---
title: "Interest Rates"
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{r setup}
# ── Source all required R modules ─────────────────────────────────────────────
# Each file handles one responsibility:
#   00_config.R   → package loads (shared across all dashboards)
#   01_helpers.R  → caching utilities and small helper functions
#   API_fred.R    → raw FRED API connector (returns tibble)
#   parse_rates.R → cleans/labels raw FRED data; provides get_rates_bundle()
#   viz_timeseries.R → plot_timeseries(), maybe_interactive()
#   viz_yieldcurve.R → plot_spread_10y_2y()
#   forecast_nextq.R → forecast_next_quarter() with 80/95% confidence bands
#   mock_data.R   → realistic fake data used when USE_MOCK = TRUE

source("R/00_config.R")
source("R/01_helpers.R")
source("R/API_fred.R")
source("R/mock_data.R")
source("R/parse_rates.R")
source("R/viz_timeseries.R")
source("R/viz_yieldcurve.R")
source("R/forecast_nextq.R")

# ── Mock vs. live data ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# USE_MOCK auto-detects whether FRED_API_KEY is available.
# When running locally without a key, mock data is used automatically.
# In CI (GitHub Actions), FRED_API_KEY is injected via Secrets → live data used.
USE_MOCK     <- Sys.getenv("FRED_API_KEY") == ""
INTERACTIVE  <- FALSE   # flip to TRUE for plotly hover/zoom
```

## Selected U.S. Interest Rates

```{r rates-load}
# Pull (or mock) all three rate series in one call.
# Output: standardized tibble with columns date, value, series_id, label, freq
rates <- get_rates_bundle(
  observation_start = "1990-01-01",
  use_mock          = USE_MOCK,
  use_cache         = TRUE,
  cache             = TRUE
)
```

```{r plot-rates}
# Chart 1: All three series on one panel (Fed Funds, 2Y, 10Y Treasury)
# Use p_rates on its own in a presentation to show just this chart.
p_rates <- plot_timeseries(
  rates,
  title = "Selected U.S. Interest Rates (FRED)",
  ylab  = "Percent"
)
maybe_interactive(p_rates, INTERACTIVE)
```

## 10Y – 2Y Treasury Spread (Yield Curve Indicator)

```{r plot-spread}
# Chart 2: Yield curve spread.
# Negative values (inversion) historically precede recessions.
# Use p_spread on its own in a presentation to show just this chart.
p_spread <- plot_spread_10y_2y(rates)
maybe_interactive(p_spread, INTERACTIVE)
```

## Fed Funds Rate: 3-Month Forecast

```{r plot-forecast}
# Chart 3: ARIMA model fit to monthly Fed Funds Rate.
# Shaded bands: inner = 80% confidence interval, outer = 95%.
# Use p_ff_forecast on its own in a presentation to show just this chart.
ff <- rates |>
  dplyr::filter(series_id == "DFF") |>
  dplyr::select(date, value)

band <- forecast_next_quarter(ff)

p_ff_forecast <- ggplot(ff, aes(date, value)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.7, na.rm = TRUE, color = "#2c7bb6") +
  # 95% confidence band (lighter)
  geom_ribbon(
    data        = band,
    aes(ymin = lo95, ymax = hi95),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    fill        = "#2c7bb6",
    alpha       = 0.15
  ) +
  # 80% confidence band (darker)
  geom_ribbon(
    data        = band,
    aes(ymin = lo80, ymax = hi80),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    fill        = "#2c7bb6",
    alpha       = 0.25
  ) +
  # Forecast mean line
  geom_line(
    data        = band,
    aes(y = mean),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    linewidth   = 0.8,
    linetype    = "dashed",
    color       = "#d7191c"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Fed Funds Rate: Model-Based Forecast (Next 3 Months)",
    subtitle = "Shaded bands: 80% and 95% confidence intervals",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Percent"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

maybe_interactive(p_ff_forecast, INTERACTIVE)
```
